event: The Intercession (Pokrov) of the Holy Virgin
symbols: outstretched veil (maphorion) — the Virgin's protective intercession covering and shielding the faithful, the spiritual mantle of mercy
crown on the Virgin — her exalted status as Queen of Heaven in Orthodox theology, the woman clothed with the sun (Revelation 12:1)
gold background — the uncreated light of divine glory and the eternal realm of heaven
gathered saints below — the communion of saints (Hebrews 12:1) sheltered under Mary's intercession
red and blue garments of Mary — the traditional iconographic colors signifying her dual nature as human (blue/earthly) and bearing divine love (red)
hierarchical composition — the heavenly order with Mary as chief intercessor between Christ and the faithful
description: An Orthodox icon in a gold frame depicting the Intercession of the Theotokos (Pokrov). The Virgin Mary stands at the top center, crowned and wearing a large dark red-blue maphorion (veil) which she extends outward with both arms to cover the gathered figures below. She stands on a cloud, elevated above the assembly. Below her, two rows of saints, bishops, and faithful -- identifiable by their various vestments including episcopal omophoria, monastic habits, and royal garments -- stand in reverent postures. The background is gold.
theological_description: The Pokrov (Protection/Intercession) icon depicts the vision traditionally associated with Saint Andrew the Fool-for-Christ, who in 10th-century Constantinople beheld the Theotokos spreading her veil (maphorion) over the congregation at the Blachernae Church, interceding for the faithful. While a post-biblical subject, its theological roots penetrate deeply into Scripture. Mary's intercessory role is grounded in her action at the wedding of Cana (John 2:1-5), where she intercedes with her Son on behalf of those in need -- 'They have no wine' -- and directs the servants to obey Him. The protective veil extends the imagery of Psalm 91:4, where God covers the faithful with His feathers and under His wings they find refuge. Orthodox theology sees Mary as the fulfillment of the 'woman clothed with the sun' in Revelation 12:1, the eschatological mother whose offspring are those 'who keep the commandments of God and hold to the testimony of Jesus' (Revelation 12:17). The gathered saints beneath her veil represent the great cloud of witnesses (Hebrews 12:1) and the communion of the Church militant and triumphant united under heavenly protection. The icon also resonates with Isaiah 4:5-6, which prophesies a canopy of glory over Zion as a shelter from storm and rain -- a protective covering that the Pokrov tradition reads as fulfilled in the Theotokos's maternal intercession for the people of God.

scripture_refs.reason: Mary's intercession at Cana -- the biblical foundation for her intercessory role depicted in the icon.
The woman clothed with the sun, crowned with stars -- the Marian reading of this eschatological figure.
The offspring of the woman who keep God's commandments -- the faithful sheltered under the veil.
God covering the faithful with His feathers -- the protective imagery translated to the Virgin's outstretched veil.
The great cloud of witnesses surrounding the faithful, represented by the gathered saints in the icon.
The canopy of glory over Zion as a shelter -- a prophetic image of divine protection over the people of God.
